codeReview.summary: Implementation is solid and meets requirements. SettingsPage has all required sections (TTS, STT, AI Model, Data Management), RAM display via platform-specific detection, and settings persistence via backend API. Code quality is consistent with existing codebase patterns.

issues.description: Duplicate import of asyncio inside async method - the asyncio module is imported at module level via 'import asyncio' in the build() method, but then re-imported in _hide_status_after_delay. While this works, it's redundant.
issues.suggestion: Remove the 'import asyncio' line inside _hide_status_after_delay since asyncio is already imported in build(). Both are inside methods so no module-level conflict exists, but consistency would be cleaner.

issues.description: The _hide_status_after_delay method imports asyncio inside the async method, creating a pattern inconsistency with other methods that use module-level imports.
issues.suggestion: Consider moving 'import asyncio' to the top of the file alongside 'import platform' for consistency, or document why method-level imports are preferred here.

issues.description: Settings save shows success message even when settings might not have actually persisted. The save_settings method only checks if 'error' is truthy, but the backend could return partial success or silent failure.
issues.suggestion: Consider validating the returned settings match what was sent, or at minimum ensure error handling is comprehensive for all HTTP status codes.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The codebase uses method-level imports for asyncio inside async methods (e.g., build() and _hide_status_after_delay), which differs from typical Python style where imports go at module level. This pattern is consistent within the codebase but not documented in AGENTS.md.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: settings_page.py:66 has 'import asyncio' inside build() method, and line 296 has another inside _hide_status_after_delay. This pattern appears in other screens too.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The flet-worker skill procedure suggests running the app with 'flet run' to verify, but the worker used pytest and ruff instead. This is appropriate since visual UI testing isn't feasible in headless environment, but the skill doesn't acknowledge this deviation path.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff verification shows: pytest tests (94 pass), ruff lint (clean), Python import check - all headless. Skill says to 'Test navigation between screens' which requires visual app.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker added a settings router and API endpoints (/api/settings, /api/settings/system) that follow existing patterns but weren't documented in services.yaml. The endpoints follow the same auth-protected pattern as profile router.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: src/routers/settings.py implements GET/PUT /api/settings with JWT auth, and GET /api/settings/system (no auth). Pattern matches src/routers/profile.py exactly.

sharedStateObservations.observation: RAM detection implementation for macOS using 'sysctl -n hw.memsize' required figuring out platform-specific commands. This knowledge could be useful for other features but isn't documented in library.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: settings_page.py get_local_ram_gb() and routers/settings.py get_system_ram_gb() both implement platform-specific RAM detection for Darwin (sysctl), Linux (/proc/meminfo), and Windows (kernel32).

summary: Review passes. The ui-settings-page feature is well-implemented with all required sections (TTS, STT, AI Model, Data Management), RAM display using platform-specific detection (macOS/Linux/Windows), and settings persistence via backend API with GET/PUT endpoints. Code follows existing patterns in the codebase and passes all tests (94) and linting. Minor code style observations (duplicate asyncio imports, method-level imports) are non-blocking and consistent with existing codebase conventions.
